name: Spinach
summary: A leafy green that's a top source of vitamin K and the eye-protecting carotenoids lutein and zeaxanthin, plus plant iron and dietary nitrate.
superfoodReason: Nutrient-dense and low-calorie, combining vitamin K, folate, plant iron, nitrate, and the highest dietary concentrations of lutein and zeaxanthin.

benefits.mechanism: Spinach is one of the richest sources of lutein and zeaxanthin — carotenoids that concentrate in the retina's macula, where they filter blue light and act as antioxidants. Higher intake is linked to slower progression of age-related macular degeneration.

citations.title: Lutein + zeaxanthin and omega-3 fatty acids for age-related macular degeneration: the AREDS2 randomized clinical trial (AREDS2 Research Group, JAMA 2013)

benefits.claim: Rich in vitamin K for bone and blood health
benefits.mechanism: Spinach is exceptionally high in vitamin K1, which the body uses to activate proteins involved in blood clotting and in binding calcium into bone.

citations.title: Vitamin K (National Institutes of Health, Office of Dietary Supplements — Health Professional Fact Sheet)

benefits.mechanism: Spinach supplies non-heme iron, the form found in plants. It's absorbed less efficiently than the heme iron in meat, but pairing spinach with a vitamin C source markedly increases how much iron the gut takes up. A 2019 review of vitamins and minerals in hair loss notes that iron deficiency — tracked by low ferritin — is a common and reversible contributor to hair shedding, so keeping iron stores up supports hair as well as red-blood-cell production.

citations.title: Iron (National Institutes of Health, Office of Dietary Supplements — Health Professional Fact Sheet)
The Role of Vitamins and Minerals in Hair Loss: A Review (Almohanna et al., Dermatol Ther 2019)

surprisingFacts.text: Spinach's iron is real, but the Popeye-era myth of it being uniquely iron-rich was overstated — pairing it with vitamin C to boost absorption matters more than the raw amount.
